note_data.title: Financial statements – Part B
keyPoints: **Balance sheet** shows the financial position of a business at a specific date
**Statement of financial position** is an alternative name for the balance sheet
Balance sheet must **balance** - Total assets = Capital + Liabilities
**Non-current assets** are assets expected to be used for more than one year
**Current assets** are assets expected to be converted to cash within one year
**Current liabilities** are debts due to be paid within one year
**Non-current liabilities** are debts due to be paid after more than one year
**Working capital** is current assets minus current liabilities
Assets are listed in order of **permanence** (non-current first, then current)
Current assets are listed in order of **liquidity** (least liquid first)

definitions.definition: A financial statement that shows the assets, liabilities and capital of a business at a specific date
Assets that are expected to be used in the business for more than one year
Assets that are expected to be converted into cash within one year
Debts that are due to be paid within one year

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Draw up the complete balance sheet with correct headings, classifications and totals
Show your working clearly and give the final answer

modelAnswer.question: Explain why working capital is important to a business. (3 marks)
modelAnswer.answer: Working capital shows the short-term liquidity position of the business because it measures current assets available to pay current liabilities. It indicates whether the business can pay its short-term debts because current assets can be converted to cash within one year. Adequate working capital ensures the business can continue operating because it can meet its immediate financial obligations.
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Indicates ability to pay short-term debts
Mark 3: Ensures business can continue operating

commonMistakes: Never say 'fixed assets' — always say 'non-current assets' for Cambridge O Level
Students often list current assets in wrong order - should be inventory, trade receivables, bank, cash
Forgetting to show subtotals for current assets, current liabilities and net current assets
Writing 'profit and loss account' instead of 'statement of profit or loss'
Not showing that the balance sheet balances by having equal totals
examinerTips: Balance sheet questions appear in every Paper 2 - worth 15-20 marks
Always show workings when calculating figures for the balance sheet
Use proper headings: 'Statement of Financial Position as at [date]'
Double-check that your balance sheet balances before moving to next question
formulas: Working Capital = Current Assets - Current Liabilities
Total Assets = Non-current Assets + Current Assets
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Vertical format balance sheet showing: Business name and date at top, then Non-current assets section, Current assets section with subtotal, Current liabilities section with subtotal, Net current assets calculation, Total net assets, and Capital section at bottom
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (8 marks) — they asked students to prepare a trading account and profit and loss account from given trial balance figures, including adjustments for depreciation and bad debts

This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P2 Q3 (6 marks) — candidates had to calculate gross profit margin and net profit margin from given financial statements and explain what these ratios indicate about business performance

This topic was examined in M/J 2022 P2 Q5 (10 marks) — students were required to prepare a balance sheet from given information, including proper classification of current and non-current assets and liabilities

This topic featured in O/N 2023 P2 Q2 (7 marks) — they tested calculation of working capital and interpretation of liquidity ratios, requiring students to advise on the business's short-term financial position

workedExample.question: From the following information, prepare a Trading Account for the year ended 31 December 2023. Opening stock $15,000; Purchases $85,000; Sales $140,000; Purchase returns $2,000; Sales returns $3,000; Closing stock $18,000; Carriage inwards $1,500. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Start with Sales figure and deduct Sales returns: $140,000 - $3,000 = $137,000 (Net Sales)
Step 2: Calculate Net Purchases: $85,000 - $2,000 + $1,500 = $84,500 (Purchases - Returns + Carriage inwards)
Step 3: Calculate Cost of Goods Sold: Opening stock + Net Purchases - Closing stock = $15,000 + $84,500 - $18,000 = $81,500
Step 4: Calculate Gross Profit: Net Sales - Cost of Goods Sold = $137,000 - $81,500 = $55,500
workedExample.answer:
TRADING ACCOUNT for year ended 31 December 2023
Sales: $140,000
Less: Sales returns: $3,000
Net Sales: $137,000
Less: Cost of Goods Sold
  Opening stock: $15,000
  Add: Purchases: $85,000
  Add: Carriage inwards: $1,500
  Less: Purchase returns: ($2,000)
  Net Purchases: $84,500
  Less: Closing stock: ($18,000)
  Cost of Goods Sold: ($81,500)
Gross Profit: $55,500
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct sales figure after returns, 2 marks for correct net purchases calculation including carriage inwards, 2 marks for correct cost of goods sold calculation, 1 mark for correct gross profit figure

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused carriage inwards with carriage outwards in the trading account' — always add carriage inwards to purchases (it's part of cost of buying goods) but treat carriage outwards as an expense in the profit and loss account

Examiner Report 2022: 'Candidates frequently placed current liabilities in the wrong section of the balance sheet' — always ensure current liabilities appear after current assets in the balance sheet, and remember that bank overdraft is a current liability, not an asset

Examiner Report 2023: 'Students often failed to show proper workings for ratio calculations' — always show your working when calculating ratios like gross profit margin (gross profit ÷ sales × 100) as method marks are awarded even if final answer is wrong
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw a standard Balance Sheet layout showing: ASSETS section with Non-current assets (Property, Equipment less Depreciation) followed by Current assets (Stock, Debtors, Bank, Cash) totaling to Total Assets. Then EQUITY AND LIABILITIES section with Capital (Opening + Profit - Drawings) followed by Current liabilities (Creditors, Accruals, Bank overdraft). The fundamental equation Assets = Capital + Liabilities must balance, with clear subtotals and a professional T-account format.
note_data.quickRecap: Financial statements Part B covers preparation of trading accounts (sales less cost of goods sold equals gross profit), profit and loss accounts (gross profit less expenses equals net profit), and balance sheets (assets must equal capital plus liabilities). Remember: carriage inwards goes in trading account, carriage outwards in P&L account. Always show workings for ratio calculations and ensure proper classification of current vs non-current items in balance sheets.
